---
title: "Introduction to MAF"
author: Erika Li, Jeanne Revilla, Mahija Mogalipuvvu, adapted from Nicole Black, Wade Boohar
date: 07/17/22
updated: 9/29/2025
---

***Deliverables***

- upload this R Notebook to your GitHub and submit the link to your Repo on Brightspace
- include ALL graphs or figures created in this assignment in a folder with your R notebook with descriptive file names.

We encourage you to work with a partner. Therefore, it is okay if your answers are the same as your partner’s as long as everyone understands it and could explain it in their own words if asked. Each person must individually push their code to Github. *At the top of your R Notebook, write the name of you and your partner(s) as a comment.*

***Complete the following coding activity and answer any following questions as comments in your R Notebook***

In this assignment, you will need to use your skills learned in class to demonstrate your understanding of categorical variables and R data structures.

*Pre-Assignment*
Load all necessary packages, read in the clinical data.csv file you have in your analysis_data folder, and instantiate the MAF_object.
```{r setup}
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath("/home1/emmashih/490_cluster/analysis_data")) 
```

```{r}
library(BiocManager)
library(maftools)
library(TCGAbiolinks)
library(ggplot2)

clinical <- read.csv("/home1/emmashih/490_cluster/analysis_data/brca_clinical_data.csv")
maf_query <- GDCquery(
  project = 'TCGA-BRCA',
  data.category = "Simple Nucleotide Variation",
  access = "open",
  data.type = "Masked Somatic Mutation",
  workflow.type = "Aliquot Ensemble Somatic Variant Merging and Masking"
)

#GDCdownload(maf_query)
setwd("/home1/emmashih/490_cluster/analysis_data")
maf <- GDCprepare(maf_query)
maf_object <- read.maf(maf = maf, clinicalData = clinical, isTCGA = TRUE)
```

It's useful to save your MAF_object, so that you don't have to go through the trouble of querying and preparing it everytime. The saveRDS function allows us to save an R object, allowing us to access it directly from our files. 
```{r}
saveRDS(maf_object, file = "/home1/emmashih/490_cluster/analysis_data/maf_object.rds")
# ex. saveRDS(MAF_object, file = "/home1/erika/490_cluster/analysis_data/maf_object.rds")
```

When you reopen a new R session and want to access your MAF_object, you can use the readRDS function to load the object back into a new variable.
```{r}
maf_object <- readRDS(file = "/home1/emmashih/490_cluster/analysis_data/maf_object.rds")
# ex. MAF_object <- readRDS(file = "/home1/erika/490_cluster/analysis_data/maf_object.rds")
```

*1*
Choose a clinical variable (or any variable from clin_rad or clin_drug) to separate your populations into two different groups and rewrite the column or create a new column with that variable as a factor. ***Do not use age or vital_status as your clinical variable.*** Hint: if your variable is continuous, you will need to determine your own cutoffs for the different levels of the factor. If your variable is categorical and has more than two possible values, choose the two that are the most common.
```{r}
maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_group <- ifelse(maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc == "Positive", "positive", ifelse(maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc == "Negative", "negative", NA)
)

maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_new <- factor(maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_new)
```


*2*
Create a co-oncoplot with the top 10-20 (you choose) most mutated genes for the two groups. Pick one that has a large discrepancy in % mutated or type of mutations between the groups and research it. 
Research it. What is the gene used for? Can you think of any reason for the discrepancy?

For the mutated gene, I chose the TP53 gene, which encodes for p53 protein. P53 protein is essential in tumor suppression, which aids in maintaining genomic stability. When a cell experiences DNA damange, p53 is activated in the cell to pause the cell cycle, allowing time for DNA repair mechanisms to work. If the damage is unfixable, p53 would trigger apoptosis to prevent ta faulty cell from dividing. Essentially, TP53 helps prevent accumulation of mutations and excessive reproduction of faulty cells, halting development of cancer. From the data, we can see that there is a significant difference in the TP53 gene being mutated 21% in ER positive patients and 81% in ER negative patients. The discrepancy likely occurs because ER-positive and ER-negative breast cancers develop through different molecular pathways. ER-negative tumors are typically more aggressive and genomically unstable, so mutations in TP53 occur more frequently to disable p53’s control of the cell cycle and allow uncontrolled growth.

```{r}
#make mask
erPosMask <- ifelse(maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc == 'Positive', T, F)
#extract barcodes from mask 
erPosBarcodes <- maf_object@clinical.data$Tumor_Sample_Barcode[erPosMask]
#use barcodes to subset/split object
erPosMaf <- subsetMaf(maf = maf_object, tsb = erPosBarcodes)

erNegMask <- ifelse(maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc == 'Negative', T, F)
erNegBarcodes <- maf_object@clinical.data$Tumor_Sample_Barcode[erNegMask]
erNegMaf <- subsetMaf(maf = maf_object, tsb = erNegBarcodes)

m1.genes = getGeneSummary(erPosMaf)[1:10]
m2.genes = getGeneSummary(erNegMaf)[1:10]
mdt = merge(m1.genes[,.(Hugo_Symbol, MutatedSamples)], m2.genes[,.(Hugo_Symbol, MutatedSamples)], by = 'Hugo_Symbol', all = TRUE)
mdt$MutatedSamples.x[is.na(mdt$MutatedSamples.x)] = 0
mdt$MutatedSamples.y[is.na(mdt$MutatedSamples.y)] = 0
mdt$max = apply(mdt[,.(MutatedSamples.x, MutatedSamples.y)], 1, max)
mdt = mdt[order(max, decreasing = TRUE)]

top_genes = mdt$Hugo_Symbol[1:20]

#co-oncoplot (positive vs negative)
coOncoplot(m1 = erPosMaf, m2 = erNegMaf, m1Name = "ER Positive", m2Name = "ER Negative", genes = top_genes, borderCol = NA)
```


*3*
Create a contingency table with your variable and chosen gene. Run a Fisher’s Exact Test between presence of mutations for that gene and your clinical variable. Create and save a mosaic plot. 
Interpret the output of the Fisher’s Exact Test in terms of the odds ratio and p-value.


Our determined p value is 2.2e-16, which at the significance threshold of 0.05, is much smaller than alpha. Thus, we have sufficient evidence that the ER status of patients affects the likelihood of a TP53 gene mutation. From the data, it is evident that having a mutated TP53 gene makes ~16.5 times more likely in negative ER patients compared to positive ER patients.

```{r}
maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc <- factor(maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc)
TP53Mut <- maf_object@data[maf_object@data$Hugo_Symbol == "TP53", ]
mutSamples<- unique(TP53Mut$Tumor_Sample_Barcode)
maf_object@clinical.data$TP53Mut <- ifelse(maf_object@clinical.data$Tumor_Sample_Barcode %in% mutSamples, "Mutated", "WT")
maf_object@clinical.data$TP53Mut <- factor(maf_object@clinical.data$TP53Mut)
contig <- table(maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc, maf_object@clinical.data$TP53Mut)
print(contig)
mosaicplot(contig)
fisher_test <- fisher.test(contig)
fisher_test$p.value
print(fisher_test)
```


*4*
Subset your maf_object based on your chosen clinical variable and create a co-lollipop plot of your chosen gene divided between the two different clinical variable possibilities. Include descriptive names on your plot.
Do you notice any difference in terms of mutations (e.g. sites, types, number) between the two populations?

While the lollipop chart displays the presence of mutations in both ER positive and EER negative group, the chart visibly shows that ER negative patients have greater number, frequency, and types of mutations. For one, there appears to be more circular colors on the side of ER neg patients, indicating greater variety of mutations. Moreover, ER Neg patients appear to have greater frequency of certain types of mutations, with a lot of the mutations containing a higher N compared to ER positive patient group despite the smaller N for ER neg. 

```{r}
erPosMaf <- subsetMaf(maf = maf_object, tsb = maf_object@clinical.data$Tumor_Sample_Barcode[maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc == 'Positive'])
erNegMaf <- subsetMaf(maf = maf_object, tsb = maf_object@clinical.data$Tumor_Sample_Barcode[maf_object@clinical.data$er_status_by_ihc == 'Negative'])
lollipopPlot2(m1 = erPosMaf, m2 = erNegMaf, m1_name = "ER Positive", m2_name = "ER Negative", gene = "TP53")
```


*5*
Create your Overall_Survival_Status column and create a mafSurvival KM plot based on mutations in your chosen gene.
Does there seem to be a difference? Hypothesize why or not based on the other analysis you did with the gene above.

This KM plot displays the Mutant group generally having a slightly lower survival probability over time, suggesting poorer outcomes compared to WT groups. The hazard ratio is 1.42 which indicates that the patients with the mutation have a 42% higher risk of death relative of those with WT gene. However the p-value is high, above 0.05, meaning the difference isn't statistically significant, and could be due to an outside random variation. 

From our generate KM plot, it appears that the mutant group has a lower survival probability. For one, the determined HR is 1:42, meaning that patients with TP53 mutations have a 42% increase in chance of death compare to those with the wildtyp TP53 gene. Despite this, our calculated pvalue appears to be greater than 0.05 (0.0858), meaning we don't have sufficient evidence to reject the null hypothesis that there isn't a difference between mortality rate of patients with wildtype gene vs. mutant gene.
```{r}
maf_object@clinical.data$survival_time <- ifelse(maf_object@clinical.data$vital_status == "Dead", maf_object@clinical.data$death_days_to, maf_object@clinical.data$last_contact_days_to)
maf_object@clinical.data$Overall_Survival_Status <- ifelse(maf_object@clinical.data$vital_status == "Dead", 1, 0)
mafSurvival(maf = maf_object,genes = "TP53",time = "survival_time",Status = "Overall_Survival_Status", isTCGA = TRUE)
```
